lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market spring manufacturers that repeatedly engineer and produce springs from purchased wire, strip, or rod to external-customer drawings and performance specifications.
lens.excluded_activities: Producers selling only standardized proprietary catalog products, vertically integrated primary-metal plants, captive operations, watch and clock spring makers classified elsewhere, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: OEM and industrial customers buy repeat production runs under drawings, material, heat-treatment, tolerance, fatigue-life, testing, packaging, and delivery specifications through purchase orders or supply agreements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code contains both custom OEM spring suppliers and standardized catalog producers. The lens retains recurring customer-specific manufacturing so the screened population has a coherent outsourced-service model.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all fabricated-metal manufacturing rather than eligible spring suppliers, and ILO reports global GenAI exposure by broad occupation. I apply higher exposure to quoting, spring-design support, purchasing, scheduling, quality records, test-data review, customer service, and administration and lower exposure to coiling, forming, heat treatment, grinding, inspection execution, packaging, and material handling. The broad BLS mix may overstate welding and machining but correctly indicates production-heavy work; GenAI-only evidence may understate machine-vision and predictive-maintenance potential.
a.rationale: Spring suppliers have engineering and quality work around materials, loads, tolerances, fatigue, and customer drawings, but a large share of labor operates, sets up, maintains, and feeds physical equipment. AI can assist information and analysis tasks without directly performing most fabrication.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from at least ten eligible spring manufacturers, excluding already automated machine cycles and measuring actual time in engineering, setup, production, quality, and administration, would materially change the range.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence covers NAICS 332 rather than 332613 or the outsourced subset.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
Existing CNC coiling, feeding, and gauging are excluded from the current opportunity.

rho.rationale: Quoting, design retrieval, scheduling, procurement, document control, and quality analytics can be implemented within five years. Legacy machines and ERP, scarce labeled failure data, safety, customer approval, cybersecurity, and the need to validate load and fatigue predictions constrain adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Pilots measuring quote time, engineering review time, schedule adherence, setup reduction, scrap, inspection false calls, accepted recommendations, and integration cost across representative plants would materially change implementation.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in lower-middle-market spring manufacturers.
Digital readiness differs sharply between high-volume automotive suppliers and low-volume specialty shops.

e.rationale: Census defines the industry by making springs from purchased wire, strip, or rod, a production structure often used for customer-specific components. Many spring firms compete on drawing, engineering, qualification, and repeat production, although standardized catalog sellers and captive suppliers do not qualify.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 122 dataset firms using customer-drawing revenue, tooling ownership, repeat production, catalog versus custom mix, captive status, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before application of the outsourced-service lens.
A custom product does not necessarily imply customer-controlled outsourced manufacturing.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not qualifying spring-manufacturer control transfers. I use this only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, internal succession, non-sale exits, institutional or multi-owner firms, customer approval, equipment condition, and key-person dependence. The broad proxy may overstate sale probability for capital-intensive suppliers but understate succession pressure in founder-led specialty shops.
s5.rationale: Independent spring suppliers can be founder-owned and transferable, but a completed control sale depends on diversified qualified programs, documented engineering, capable staff, usable equipment, and limited dependence on the exiting owner.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal records, UCC and ownership changes, and interviews with spring-sector brokers, owners, and lenders covering eligible firms over the prior five years would materially change the probability.
caveats: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age and succession pressure do not establish sale intent or transfer completion.

q.rationale: Qualification, tooling, fatigue performance, delivery reliability, and switching cost can preserve savings within customer programs. Annual cost-down demands, competitive bidding, steel surcharges, should-cost analysis, and concentrated OEM procurement pass visible productivity gains to customers.
q.change_evidence: A contract and purchase-order sample showing term, cost-down clauses, metal pass-through, tooling ownership, productivity sharing, renewal cadence, and historical concessions would materially change retention.
caveats: No public contract dataset measures pass-through for eligible spring suppliers.
Automotive and other high-volume OEM programs may have lower retention than low-volume critical applications.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production measures all spring and wire products at NAICS 3326, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible 332613 custom spring services. The index declined from 88.1678 in 2021 to 66.7493 in 2025. I translate that broad weakness into modest base contraction but allow recovery for qualified reshoring and replacement programs. The proxy may overstate weakness because other fabricated-wire products are included and may understate import pressure in standardized springs.
d5.rationale: Springs remain physical components in vehicles, machinery, aerospace, medical devices, appliances, electronics, and industrial equipment, creating replacement and new-production demand. Current domestic output is weak, however, and customer cycles, imports, electrification-driven redesign, and inventory corrections can reduce volume.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-specific unit forecasts, bookings, backlog, import penetration, vehicle and machinery build plans, and product-level shipment histories for eligible spring firms would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The index combines springs with other fabricated wire products and includes firms outside the lens.
End markets differ sharply in cyclicality and qualification barriers.
Historical output is not a direct five-year forecast.

o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical coiling, forming, heat treatment, finishing, testing, packaging, and accountable conformance to load and fatigue requirements. Software can improve design and coordination but cannot supply the spring; displacement is mainly insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, or mechanism redesign.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, design changes eliminating springs, additive or alternative-mechanism qualification, and evidence of fully autonomous approved production would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Operator requirement can persist while production shifts offshore or to larger suppliers.
Product elimination and import substitution are reflected mainly in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Custom spring manufacturing fits the outsourced-service lens relatively naturally because customers repeatedly buy drawing- and performance-specific parts. AI can improve engineering and administrative flow, but most labor remains physical and broad domestic spring and wire output has contracted sharply.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist quote preparation, design retrieval, material selection, scheduling, purchasing, test-data review, quality documentation, maintenance triage, and customer service. Coiling, setup, forming, heat treatment, grinding, inspection execution, and packaging require machinery and physical accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Qualified programs, tooling, fatigue performance, and reliable delivery create switching cost. OEM cost-down clauses, competitive rebids, steel indices, and should-cost models can transfer visible savings, making contract structure decisive.
narrative.firm_availability: A meaningful share of spring firms may be customer-specific suppliers, and founder succession can create transactions. Transferability still depends on customer diversification, program approvals, engineering records, equipment condition, workforce depth, and limited owner dependence.
narrative.demand_durability: Springs remain necessary in many physical systems, but domestic volume is exposed to imports, customer build cycles, product redesign, electrification, and inventory corrections. Critical low-volume springs should be more durable than standardized high-volume products.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The case weakens if the target is a catalog seller, depends on one OEM program, lacks digital production data, needs capital automation rather than AI, or competes in declining standardized products. Fatigue liability, quality escapes, and customer cost-down expectations are material.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat customer-specific programs and qualification create sticky demand with improvable engineering, quoting, and quality workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Production labor is physical and broad domestic spring and wire output has declined, limiting demand confidence.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: Spring Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines 332613 as manufacturing springs from purchased wire, strip, or rod and excludes watch and clock springs and vertically integrated primary-metal production.

sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 fabricated-metal employment of 56,060 press operators, 67,090 production supervisors, 101,640 machinists, 125,250 team assemblers, and 112,520 welders.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: Policies to Manage the Transition
sources.supports: ILO reports 24% of clerical tasks at high automation exposure and 58% at medium exposure, while other occupational groups have only 1% to 4% highly exposed and no more than 25% medium-exposed tasks.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Spring and Wire Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: The Federal Reserve annual index for NAICS 3326 declined from 88.1678 in 2021 to 66.7493 in 2025, with 2017 equal to 100.

sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers
sources.supports: BLS projects overall employment of metal and plastic machine workers to decline 7% from 2024 to 2034 and reports fabricated metal product manufacturing employs 25% of the occupation.
